# Transcrição: 08-Powerful Rapport Technique

- **Fonte:** 08-Powerful Rapport Technique
- **Duração:** 09:39

## [00:00 - 03:01]

In the world of magic, magicians have to learn how to build rapport with people very fast. Very early in my magic career I was a roving magician and a roving magician is a magician that will enter a party, sometimes filled with hundreds or even thousands of people and we have to perform for them all evening, maybe three to four hours. And what that means is we're going to be meeting new groups of people consistently all night long. And that's why we have to learn how to build rapport very fast. Because you're moving into a group, they don't know who you are and you just walk in, you perform for them for about five to six minutes, you leave that group, you go to another group straight away. So to me when I was performing roving magic, I again had to learn how to build rapport very very fast. And the technique that magicians would use is a technique called matching and mirroring. You've probably heard about this before and if you haven't, I can't wait to explain to you the power behind matching and mirroring. Now most people when they learn about matching and mirroring, they're learning about it from a body language aspect. Oh, you match and mirror somebody's visual image. So if someone is standing very confidently and they're standing very tall and they've also got very good posture, very strong body language and big body language, especially with their hand gestures, if you want to connect with that person more, you've got to make sure you adjust your posture as well and use large body language too. Because that way they will go, oh, you're kind of the same as me. You're similar to me. You're familiar to me. And then all of a sudden they'll connect with you faster. Whereas if you met someone who had smaller body language and then you walk in and you're being all big and rowdy, they're not going to connect with you. So that's the importance of matching and mirroring when it comes to body language. I'm talking about it from a different point of view. Before we go into what I'm going to talk about when it comes to matching and mirroring, I want to show you a clip. Magicians actually use matching and mirroring for magic tricks as well. I want to show you the power behind matching and mirroring. Check this piece of magic out. Here we go. I need you to take about half the cards for me. About half. And then hold them in your hand. Good. And I take the remaining half. Now I want you to spread the cards out just to get a bit of feel. Feel how the cards feel. Just spread them out and then close them. Spread them out and then close them. Because they're very slippery, don't drop them. And then spread them out and then close them. Good. Now I'm going to teach you how to deal. So when you're dealing cards, deal onto the mat here. Just do one at a time. Yep. That's exactly as I'm doing. Do one at a time. And here, free choice. You can stop any time you'd like. It's up to you. Okay? Stop any time. Good. Free choice? That was fair? Free choice. Good. So now I want you to shuffle the cards. However, yeah, above the table. Okay, thank you. Good. You can stop any time you'd like to. That's what I want to teach you. Good. Free choice. So I want you to hold the cards now in two packs and you're right on your left.

Now I mentioned before the value of matching and mirroring when it comes to body language. Now of course, I just want to say here, it's not about meeting someone and then immediately just copying what they're doing. You can't just meet someone and go, oh, they just, oh, they folded their arms. Oh, leaning on the table. Oh, going to lean on the table. Oh, crossing of the legs. Oh, hand to the chin. No, no, no, no. You're going to seem like a creep. You have to be able to learn how to do this in a very subtle manner. ## [06:01 - 09:02]

There's a famous psychiatrist named Dr. Milton Erickson, who was a brilliant psychiatrist in that he understood how to match and mirror people when it came to their vocal foundations. For example, if he had someone that was very kind of alpha and very loud and abrupt and a patient that came in and they kind of said, oh, I don't even know why I'm here. This is a complete waste of my time. He wouldn't use his natural foundation and say, oh, well, that's okay. Maybe you should give it a try because this person's got to be like, no, this is stupid. You're stupid. But what Dr. Milton Erickson actually did was he matched them and said, look, I know you don't know why you're here, but you're here anyway. You drove 30 minutes to be here. You might as well give this a go. And the other person's going to like, okay, okay, I kind of like, I don't know why, but I kind of like this person. I don't know why, but I like this person. Now the same goes if someone comes in and they're very shy, reserved and quiet. When they come in and they say, look, Dr. I don't know why I'm here. I don't think I need to be here, but my family insists that I come and look, I really have other things to do. I need to go. And then he would do the same thing, the doctor. He would say, look, I know you feel like this could be a waste of your time. And often people don't even, you know, look, you're here anyway. Why don't we just sit down and have a chat? It's not a formal session. Let's just have a bit of a chat. And all of a sudden this person would feel like, I don't know why I kind of feel connected to this person. I do this all the time, especially when I run workshops. I mean, I'm teaching public speaking. This is one of the most frightening things for a lot of people. So I have to be very gentle with people, but at the same time, I like to push my students very far out of their comfort zone. But the only way they are going to let me push them out of their comfort zone, the only way they're going to trust me enough for me to push them is if we have lots of rapport and if we feel connected. So the only way I'm able to push my students is because I build lots of rapport with them by using this technique. This is one of the techniques that I use. Again, this is something that you have to practice. You don't want to just meet people and immediately try to mime them. Because then again, it comes across as inauthentic. It comes across as a bit strange. But also understand that the only way to get good at this is sometimes you've got to start by being a little bit bad at it. It's one of my famous quotes, as some would say, is that the price you must pay to become great is you must first be a little bit shit. I know, right? How eloquent is that? How eloquent then? You're going to get quoted by Time's Magazine one day. But look, I want you to really understand the value behind this. This is how you build rapport fast. And trust me, as a magician who had to walk into a room with hundreds of people and connect with them almost immediately, this is a technique that works. Now if you want to practice this, find environments that are low risk, low risk environments.

## [09:02 - 09:39]

Go to maybe a networking function that's outside of your industry so you don't accidentally meet your boss there or you meet somebody that might know you from your industry. Go to an improv class. Go to a class where they're just doing improv theater activities and just have fun trying to match and mirror people. So find low risk environments. Obviously, don't go into your job tomorrow and then walk into your boss's office and start to build rapport with your boss because you want to pay rise and you walk in, you're like, hello, boss. And you sit the same way he's sitting and then you match the same way he's speaking because you're probably going to get fired. So don't do that. I hope you can see now from this example in this video the power behind matching and mirroring.
